questions.question: Which ACL type should be placed CLOSEST to the destination of traffic?
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs filter only by source IP, so placing them near the source may inadvertently block traffic to valid destinations. They go near the DESTINATION. Extended ACLs should be near the SOURCE.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q17; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 34: ACLs

questions.question: What is the implicit rule at the end of every Cisco IOS ACL?
questions.explanation: Every Cisco ACL ends with an implicit 'deny any'. If no ACE matches the traffic, it is dropped silently. This is why every ACL must have at least one permit statement.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q14; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: An ACL is applied to an interface but the referenced ACL number does not exist yet. What happens to traffic?
options: The command fails with an error
Inbound traffic is denied, outbound is permitted
questions.explanation: When no ACL is applied to an interface, it implicitly permits all traffic. The ip access-group command is accepted even if the ACL doesn't exist — the interface behaves as permit all until the ACL is created.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q15; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: What does the 'established' keyword do in an extended ACL?
options: Permits TCP traffic only if the SYN flag is set
Permits TCP traffic only if the ACK bit is set (not the initial SYN)
Establishes a persistent permit entry in the ACL
questions.explanation: 'established' matches TCP packets where the ACK flag is set — meaning the session was already initiated. The initial SYN packet (ACK=0) is blocked. This allows outbound sessions while preventing unsolicited inbound connections.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q16 and Q52; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: In a Cisco running-config, 'enable secret 5' and 'enable password 7' use different encryption. What does the type number mean?
options: 5 = two-way reversible algorithm; 7 = one-way MD5 hash
5 = one-way MD5 hash (secure); 7 = two-way reversible algorithm (weak)
questions.explanation: Type 5 = MD5 hash (one-way, cannot be reversed). Type 7 = Vigenere cipher (two-way, easily cracked). Always use 'enable secret' over 'enable password'. Modern IOS supports Type 8 (PBKDF2-SHA256) and Type 9 (scrypt).
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q23; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 6

questions.question: If the 'access-group' command is configured on an interface but no access-list has been created, which is most correct?
options: The command will execute and deny all traffic
The command will execute and permit all traffic in and out
The command will execute and deny all traffic in and out
questions.explanation: When ip access-group references a non-existent ACL, IOS accepts the command without error and permits all traffic — the interface behaves as if no ACL is applied. The ACL only takes effect once it is created.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q1; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: The statement 'access-list 101 deny tcp 10.1.8.0 0.0.7.255 192.16.5.0 0.0.0.255' would deny traffic from which source subnets?
questions.explanation: Wildcard mask 0.0.7.255 means: ignore the last 3 bits of the 3rd octet and all 8 bits of the 4th. Starting at 10.1.8.0, this matches subnets 10.1.8.0 through 10.1.15.255 — so both 10.1.8.0 and 10.1.15.0 fall within this range.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q4; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: What is the correct formula to calculate the ACL wildcard mask for a subnet?
options: Wildcard = subnet mask inverted at the network bits only
questions.explanation: Wildcard mask = 255.255.255.255 − subnet mask. Example: subnet 255.255.255.224 → wildcard = 0.0.0.31. In a wildcard mask: 0 = check this bit (must match), 1 = ignore this bit (wildcard). The shortcut: 'host' = 0.0.0.0, 'any' = 255.255.255.255.
questions.source_ref: Boson Software - Cisco IP Access-List Wildcard Masks (1999); Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Which ACL wildcard mask matches ONLY a single specific host?
questions.explanation: Wildcard 0.0.0.0 means: check ALL 32 bits — every bit must match exactly. This matches only that one host. In Cisco IOS, 'host 10.1.1.1' is shorthand for '10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0'. Wildcard 255.255.255.255 = 'any' (ignore all bits).
questions.source_ref: Boson Software - Cisco IP Access-List Wildcard Masks (1999); Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 34

questions.question: What does the 'switchport port-security maximum 2' command enforce?
options: Allows only 2 VLANs on the port
Limits the port to a maximum of 2 learned MAC addresses
questions.explanation: Port security limits the number of source MAC addresses allowed on a port. 'maximum 2' allows at most 2 MAC addresses. When violated, default action is 'shutdown' (err-disabled). Actions: shutdown (default), restrict (drops + logs), protect (drops silently). Verify with 'show port-security interface'.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 27 Port Security; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 49

questions.question: Which Cisco IOS command configures SSH access and restricts VTY lines to SSH only?
options: ip ssh enable → line vty 0 4 → transport input ssh
service ssh → line vty 0 4 → allow ssh
crypto key generate rsa → ip domain-name ccna.lab → line vty 0 4 → transport input ssh
questions.explanation: SSH setup on Cisco IOS: 1) 'ip domain-name [domain]' — required for key gen; 2) 'crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048' — generates RSA keypair; 3) 'ip ssh version 2'; 4) 'username admin secret [pass]'; 5) 'line vty 0 4 → transport input ssh → login local'. This replaces insecure Telnet.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 33 Device Security; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 43

questions.question: What is the number range for Standard IP Access Control Lists on Cisco routers?
questions.explanation: Standard IP ACLs use numbers 1–99. Extended IP ACLs use 100–199. Standard ACLs filter based on source address only. Extended ACLs filter on source, destination, protocol, and port.

questions.question: What is the number range for Extended IP Access Control Lists?
questions.explanation: Extended IP ACLs use numbers 100–199. They can filter on source address, destination address, protocol (TCP, UDP, ICMP, EIGRP, etc.), and port number. Standard ACLs (1–99) filter on source address only.

questions.question: All IP access control lists end with an implicit statement. What is it?
questions.explanation: All ACLs end with an implicit 'deny any' statement. If a packet does not match any explicit permit or deny entry in the list, it is automatically discarded. This is why you must always include a 'permit' statement for traffic you want to allow.

questions.question: What does 'inbound' mean when applied to an access list on a router interface?
options: Packets going OUT from the router to the network
Packets coming INTO the router from the network through that interface
questions.explanation: Inbound ACL = packets ENTERING the router FROM the network (through the specified interface). Outbound ACL = packets EXITING the router to the network. Think from the router's perspective, not the interface's. Apply with: ip access-group {number} in

questions.question: In an IP access list, the keyword 'any' is equivalent to which wildcard mask specification?
questions.explanation: The keyword 'any' in an ACL is equivalent to '0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255' — any source address with every bit being a 'don't care' bit. The wildcard 255.255.255.255 means ALL bits are ignored, matching any IP address.

questions.question: Which command correctly applies access list 101 as an outbound filter on interface FastEthernet 0/1?
questions.explanation: The command to apply an ACL to an interface is 'ip access-group {number} {in|out}'. Applied under interface configuration mode. Number 101 indicates an Extended IP ACL.

questions.question: What does an Extended IP ACL filter on that a Standard IP ACL cannot?
options: Destination IP address, protocol type, and port number
questions.explanation: Standard IP ACLs filter on source address only. Extended IP ACLs can also filter on destination address, protocol (TCP, UDP, ICMP, EIGRP, etc.), and port number (e.g., 'eq www' for port 80). Example: access-list 101 permit tcp any 10.3.0.25 eq www
questions.source_ref: Talbert talbert5.htm Extended IP Access Lists

questions.question: Which 802.11 wireless standard operates exclusively in the 5 GHz band?
questions.explanation: 802.11a operates exclusively in the 5 GHz band at up to 54 Mbps. 802.11b operates at 2.4 GHz (up to 11 Mbps). 802.11g operates at 2.4 GHz (up to 54 Mbps). 802.11n operates on both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz (up to 600 Mbps).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 56: Wireless; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is the function of an Access Point (AP) in infrastructure mode wireless networking?
options: To route traffic between different IP subnets
To act as a central hub connecting wireless clients to the wired network
To encrypt all wireless traffic using WPA3
To assign IP addresses to wireless clients
questions.explanation: In infrastructure mode, an Access Point (AP) acts as the central connection point, bridging wireless clients to the wired network (Distribution System). Wireless clients associate with the AP, which connects to the wired Ethernet LAN. In ad-hoc mode, devices communicate peer-to-peer without an AP.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 56: Wireless; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'banner motd' command on a Cisco router?
options: To configure a Message of the Day warning displayed to users who connect
To configure the console line timeout
questions.explanation: 'banner motd #...#' configures a Message of the Day (MOTD) banner displayed to anyone who accesses the router. This is used for legal warning notices. Syntax: conf t → banner motd # [message text] #. The delimiter (#) marks the end of the message.

questions.question: Which configuration sets up a password for Telnet/SSH (VTY) access to a Cisco router?
options: conf t → line vty 0 4 → login → password {password}
questions.explanation: VTY (Virtual Terminal) lines handle Telnet/SSH access. The sequence is: conf t → line vty 0 4 → login → password {password}. 'enable secret' secures privileged mode. 'service password-encryption' encrypts all stored passwords but doesn't set VTY access.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about IP access control lists are TRUE? Choose all that apply.
options: ACLs should have at least one permit statement
Every ACL has an implicit deny-all at the end
ACLs are processed top-down, first match wins
questions.explanation: All three statements are true: (1) Every ACL needs at least one permit, otherwise the implicit deny blocks all traffic. (2) There is an implicit 'deny any' at the end of every ACL. (3) ACLs are processed top-to-bottom; as soon as a match is found, that action is taken and no further entries are checked.

questions.question: An interface has the 'ip access-group 101 in' command applied, but ACL 101 has not been created. What happens to traffic?
options: An error message is displayed and the command is rejected
All traffic is denied in and out
All traffic is permitted in and out
All traffic is denied inbound only
questions.explanation: If 'ip access-group' is applied but the ACL doesn't exist, all traffic is PERMITTED. Think of it as a security guard that hasn't arrived at the gate yet — no guard means no filtering. Only when an ACL exists AND is applied does the implicit deny take effect.

questions.question: What does the 'established' keyword do in an Extended ACL entry like 'access-list 101 permit tcp any 172.16.0.0 0.0.255.255 established'?
options: Permits all TCP traffic regardless of source
Only permits TCP packets that are RETURN traffic (ACK/RST bit set), blocking new connections from outside
Permits only the first SYN packet of a TCP session
questions.explanation: The 'established' keyword permits TCP packets that have the ACK or RST bit set — meaning they are part of an already-established session (return traffic). New TCP connections (initial SYN only, no ACK) are blocked. This allows internal hosts to initiate outbound connections while preventing unsolicited inbound connections.

questions.question: Where should Standard and Extended IP ACLs be placed for best practice?
options: Standard: close to source; Extended: close to destination
Standard: close to destination; Extended: close to source
Both should be placed close to the source
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs filter on SOURCE only — placing them close to the source could accidentally block traffic to other destinations. Place STANDARD ACLs close to the DESTINATION. Extended ACLs filter on source AND destination — they can be precise, so place EXTENDED ACLs close to the SOURCE to avoid unnecessary traffic traversal.

questions.question: The 'line console 0' command can be used to:
options: Establish a Telnet session with a remote router
Set a login password on the console terminal
Set a password to restrict access to privileged EXEC mode
Set a password for incoming VTY (Telnet) sessions
questions.explanation: 'line console 0' enters console line configuration mode. From here you can set: 'password {password}' and 'login' to require a password on direct console access. For Telnet/SSH access use 'line vty 0 4'. For privileged mode use 'enable password' or 'enable secret'.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'enable password' and 'enable secret' on Cisco routers?
options: No difference — both encrypt the password the same way
'enable secret' uses MD5 hash (one-way, secure); 'enable password' is stored in cleartext
'enable secret' is only for Telnet; 'enable password' is for console
'enable password' uses SHA-256; 'enable secret' uses MD5
questions.explanation: 'enable secret' stores the password as an MD5 hash (one-way, Type 5) — it cannot be reversed. 'enable password' stores in cleartext (visible with 'show running-config') unless 'service password-encryption' is applied (Type 7, which is weak/reversible). 'enable secret' always takes precedence over 'enable password' if both are configured.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_CramGuide.htm section 21; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 5

questions.question: What does 'service password-encryption' do on a Cisco router?
options: Encrypts all passwords using MD5 (one-way hash)
Applies Type 7 (Vigenere cipher) weak encryption to all plaintext passwords in the config
Enables SSH encryption for all Telnet sessions
questions.explanation: 'service password-encryption' applies Cisco's proprietary Type 7 (reversible Vigenere cipher) encryption to all plaintext passwords in the running config (console, vty, enable password). This prevents casual shoulder-surfing but is NOT truly secure — Type 7 is easily decoded. Use 'enable secret' (Type 5 MD5) for privileged mode security.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 5: Password Security; Wendell Odom OCG

questions.question: What is 802.1X used for in network security?
options: Port-based Network Access Control — authenticating devices before granting network access
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1X provides port-based Network Access Control (PNAC). When a device connects to a switch port, 802.1X requires authentication via EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) before the port is opened. It uses a Supplicant (client), Authenticator (switch), and Authentication Server (RADIUS). Commonly used in enterprise Wi-Fi and wired networks.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 50: 802.1X; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What are the minimum steps to configure SSH access on a Cisco router?
options: Set hostname, set domain name, generate RSA keys, configure VTY lines for SSH
Only configure vty lines with `transport input ssh`
Install SSL certificate and configure HTTPS
Set enable secret and configure VTY with password
questions.explanation: SSH configuration requires: (1) `hostname <name>` — router needs a unique name; (2) `ip domain-name <domain>` — needed for RSA key generation; (3) `crypto key generate rsa` — creates the RSA key pair (2048 bits recommended); (4) `line vty 0 4` then `transport input ssh` and `login local`; (5) create a local user with `username <x> secret <pw>`.

questions.question: What are the three port security violation modes in Cisco IOS?
questions.explanation: Port security violation modes: Protect — silently drops frames from unknown MACs, no notification. Restrict — drops frames AND increments violation counter, sends syslog. Shutdown (default) — puts port into err-disabled state, sends syslog/SNMP trap. The port must be manually re-enabled after shutdown: `shutdown` then `no shutdown`.

questions.question: What is the difference between RADIUS and TACACS+ for AAA?
options: RADIUS=TCP 49, encrypts entire packet; TACACS+=UDP 1812/1813, encrypts only password
RADIUS=UDP 1812/1813, encrypts only password; TACACS+=TCP 49, encrypts entire packet
Both use UDP; RADIUS is Cisco proprietary while TACACS+ is open standard
RADIUS is for authentication only; TACACS+ is for authorization only
questions.explanation: RADIUS: open standard, uses UDP (1812=auth, 1813=accounting), only encrypts the password. TACACS+: Cisco-developed, uses TCP port 49, encrypts the ENTIRE packet (more secure). TACACS+ separates Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting into independent services. RADIUS combines authentication and authorization.

questions.question: What is the difference between an IDS and an IPS in terms of traffic handling?
options: IDS blocks malicious traffic inline; IPS only monitors and alerts
IDS monitors and alerts (out-of-band); IPS sits inline and can block traffic in real-time
IDS is hardware-based; IPS is software-based
They are identical; IDS is the older name for IPS
questions.explanation: IDS (Intrusion Detection System) operates out-of-band — it receives a copy of traffic, analyzes it, and generates alerts but CANNOT block traffic. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is inline — all traffic passes through it, allowing real-time blocking of detected threats. IPS adds latency; IDS does not.

questions.question: What does 'AAA' stand for in network security, and which Cisco service implements it?
options: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting — implemented via RADIUS or TACACS+
Authentication, Availability, Access — implemented via ACLs
Authorization, Auditing, Alerts — implemented via Syslog
Access, Administration, Accounting — implemented via SNMP
questions.explanation: AAA = Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what are you allowed to do?), Accounting (what did you do?). Cisco implements AAA via RADIUS (open standard, UDP 1812/1813) or TACACS+ (Cisco, TCP 49). Configured with `aaa new-model`, then `aaa authentication login default group radius local` etc.

questions.question: What is a common Layer 2 attack and how does Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) mitigate it?
options: STP manipulation — DAI blocks BPDU frames
ARP spoofing/poisoning — DAI validates ARP packets against a DHCP snooping binding table
MAC flooding — DAI limits MAC addresses per port
VLAN hopping — DAI enforces 802.1Q native VLANs
questions.explanation: ARP spoofing is a Layer 2 attack where a host sends fake ARP replies to poison ARP caches, redirecting traffic through the attacker (man-in-the-middle). DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection) mitigates this by checking ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table (IP-to-MAC mappings). Ports without valid DHCP bindings are untrusted by default.

questions.question: What is DHCP snooping and what attacks does it prevent?
options: Monitors DHCP traffic for unusual patterns; prevents DoS attacks
A Layer 2 security feature that filters DHCP messages — allows only DHCP servers on trusted ports; prevents rogue DHCP servers
Encrypts DHCP messages to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks
Blocks all DHCP broadcasts on untrusted VLANs
questions.explanation: DHCP Snooping is a Layer 2 security feature that distinguishes between trusted (uplink to legitimate DHCP server) and untrusted (client-facing) ports. DHCP server messages (OFFER, ACK) from untrusted ports are dropped, preventing rogue DHCP servers. It also builds an IP-MAC-port binding table used by DAI and IP Source Guard.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of secure MAC addresses configurable with port security, and what command sets it?
options: Maximum is 128; configured with `switchport port-security maximum 128`
No maximum; limited only by switch MAC table
Maximum is 1 by default; configured with `switchport port-security mac-address max <n>`
questions.explanation: Port security default maximum is 1 MAC address per port. You can increase it: `switchport port-security maximum <1-132>`. The default violation action is shutdown. To enable: `switchport port-security`. To verify: `show port-security interface <interface>`. Sticky MACs: `switchport port-security mac-address sticky` learns and saves MACs to running-config.

questions.question: What is a VLAN hopping attack and what are two ways to mitigate it?
options: Attacker sends oversized frames; mitigation = jumbo frames
Attacker uses switch spoofing or double-tagging to access other VLANs; mitigation = disable DTP and change native VLAN from default (1)
Attacker ARP-poisons the default gateway; mitigation = DAI
Attacker floods MAC table; mitigation = port security
questions.explanation: VLAN hopping attacks: (1) Switch spoofing — attacker's port negotiates trunk (DTP). Mitigate: `switchport mode access` + `switchport nonegotiate` on access ports. (2) Double tagging — attacker sends 802.1Q-tagged frame with native VLAN, which gets forwarded to another VLAN. Mitigate: change native VLAN to an unused VLAN (not VLAN 1): `switchport trunk native vlan 999`.

questions.question: What command shows whether port security is enabled on a switch interface and its current state?
questions.explanation: `show port-security interface <if>` displays: port security status (Enabled/Disabled), violation mode, maximum/current MAC addresses, violation count, and last violating MAC. `show port-security address` shows the secure MAC table. An err-disabled port (after shutdown violation) shows as 'Secure-shutdown'.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 802.1X port-based authentication?
options: Encrypts all traffic on the access port using AES
Requires devices to authenticate to an AAA server (RADIUS) before gaining network access through a switch port
Assigns VLANs based on MAC address source
Provides physical layer authentication via cable type
questions.explanation: 802.1X uses EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) for port-based NAC (Network Access Control). Three roles: Supplicant (end device with 802.1X client), Authenticator (switch/AP), Authentication Server (RADIUS). Until authenticated, the port only passes EAP frames. After auth, normal access is granted. Can dynamically assign VLANs based on auth result.

questions.question: What are the wireless security protocols in order from weakest to strongest?
questions.explanation: Wireless security evolution: WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) — RC4 encryption, easily cracked, considered broken. WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access) — TKIP encryption, transitional, also vulnerable. WPA2 — AES-CCMP encryption, current standard (IEEE 802.11i), uses PSK or 802.1X/EAP. WPA3 — latest standard, SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) replaces PSK, forward secrecy, more secure.

questions.question: What is the difference between a virus, a worm, and a trojan horse?
options: All three are identical; just different names
Virus=requires host file, needs user action; Worm=self-replicates over network without user action; Trojan=disguised as legitimate software
Virus=spreads over network; Worm=requires email; Trojan=requires USB
questions.explanation: Virus: attaches to legitimate files/programs; requires user action (opening file) to spread; damages or modifies files. Worm: self-replicating, spreads automatically over network without user interaction; exploits vulnerabilities. Trojan horse: appears to be legitimate/useful software but contains malicious code; doesn't self-replicate but may open backdoors.

questions.question: What is a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack?
options: An attack where the attacker floods the network with broadcast traffic
An attack where the attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties to intercept or alter communications
An attack where the attacker guesses passwords repeatedly
An attack where the attacker sends oversized packets to crash a host
questions.explanation: A MITM attack intercepts communications between two parties without their knowledge. The attacker may eavesdrop on or modify the data. Common MITM techniques include ARP spoofing (redirects traffic at Layer 2) and DNS spoofing. Mitigation: encryption (TLS/HTTPS), certificate validation, DAI (for ARP MITM), and DNSSEC.

questions.question: What is social engineering in the context of network security?
options: Engineering tools that make network management easier for social interactions
Manipulating people through psychological tactics to reveal confidential information or perform actions that compromise security
A method of configuring network devices via social media APIs
A collaborative approach to network design involving multiple teams
questions.explanation: Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Common techniques: Phishing (fraudulent emails/websites), Vishing (voice phishing via phone), Pretexting (creating a fabricated scenario), Baiting (offering something enticing), Tailgating/Piggybacking (following authorized person through secure door). User awareness training is the primary defense.

questions.question: How do you calculate the wildcard mask for a subnet? What is the wildcard for 255.255.254.0?
questions.explanation: Formula: Wildcard mask = 255.255.255.255 − subnet mask. For 255.255.254.0: 255-255=0, 255-255=0, 255-254=1, 255-0=255 → Wildcard = 0.0.1.255. This means: match first two octets exactly, allow any value in byte 3 up to 1, allow any host byte. Covers two subnets: x.x.even.0 and x.x.odd.0.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security architecture?
options: A military-grade secure network zone with no internet access
A perimeter network segment that hosts public-facing servers while isolating them from the internal network
A VLAN dedicated to network management traffic
A firewall configuration that blocks all inbound traffic
questions.explanation: A DMZ is a perimeter network segment between the internet and the internal network, typically created using two firewalls. Public-facing servers (web, email, DNS) are placed in the DMZ — they're accessible from the internet but isolated from internal systems. If a DMZ server is compromised, attackers can't directly reach internal resources. Traffic flow: Internet → outer firewall → DMZ → inner firewall → internal.

questions.question: What is the difference between WPA2 Personal (PSK) and WPA2 Enterprise?
options: WPA2 Personal uses AES; WPA2 Enterprise uses TKIP
WPA2 Personal uses a shared password (Pre-Shared Key) for all users; WPA2 Enterprise requires individual authentication via 802.1X/EAP with a RADIUS server
WPA2 Enterprise is less secure but easier to deploy
WPA2 Personal is for businesses; WPA2 Enterprise is for home users
questions.explanation: WPA2 Personal (PSK): single shared key configured on all clients and the AP — simple but risky (one user leaves, all must change key). WPA2 Enterprise: each user authenticates individually using 802.1X/EAP to a RADIUS server — unique per-user credentials, centralized management, can revoke individual access without changing the entire network key. Both use AES-CCMP encryption. Enterprise is required for corporate environments.

questions.question: In 802.1X port-based authentication, what are the three roles?
questions.explanation: 802.1X uses three roles: Supplicant (end device requesting access), Authenticator (network device, typically a switch, that controls port access), and Authentication Server (typically a RADIUS server that validates credentials). EAP carries authentication between supplicant and server through the authenticator.

questions.question: What does the `sticky` keyword do in a port security configuration?
options: Prevents the port from going to err-disable state
Dynamically learns MAC addresses and saves them to the running-config
Allows only statically configured MAC addresses
Enables the port to accept unlimited MAC addresses
questions.explanation: The `switchport port-security mac-address sticky` command enables sticky learning: the switch dynamically learns MAC addresses on the port and converts them to sticky secure addresses, saving them to the running-config (and startup-config if saved). This is easier than manually typing MAC addresses.

questions.question: What type of attack involves an attacker intercepting and potentially altering communication between two parties without their knowledge?
questions.explanation: A Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack occurs when an attacker secretly relays and possibly alters communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly. ARP spoofing is a common technique used for MITM attacks on local networks.

questions.question: What is phishing?
options: Flooding a network with traffic to cause a denial of service
Social engineering via email or fake websites to steal credentials
Scanning a network to discover open ports and services
questions.explanation: Phishing is a social engineering attack where attackers send deceptive emails or create fake websites to trick users into revealing sensitive information like passwords or credit card numbers. Spear phishing targets specific individuals. Vishing uses voice calls.

questions.question: What is the difference between a site-to-site VPN and a remote-access VPN?
options: Site-to-site uses SSL; remote-access uses IPsec
Site-to-site connects two networks permanently; remote-access connects individual users to the network
Site-to-site is less secure than remote-access
questions.explanation: A site-to-site VPN creates a permanent encrypted tunnel between two networks (e.g., branch office to HQ). A remote-access VPN allows individual users (telecommuters) to securely connect to the corporate network from any location. Both typically use IPsec or SSL/TLS.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of a stateful firewall over a stateless packet filter?
options: It operates faster because it doesn't maintain any tables
It tracks the state of TCP connections and allows return traffic automatically
It can filter based on application content
questions.explanation: A stateful firewall maintains a connection state table and tracks active TCP/UDP sessions. It automatically allows return traffic for established connections without needing explicit rules. A stateless packet filter inspects each packet independently without context. Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW) add application-level inspection (Layer 7).

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of DHCP snooping on a switch?
options: To speed up DHCP address allocation
To prevent rogue DHCP servers from assigning addresses on the network
To enable DHCP relay across VLANs
To limit the number of DHCP leases per VLAN
questions.explanation: DHCP snooping protects against rogue DHCP servers. It classifies switch ports as trusted (uplinks to legitimate DHCP servers) or untrusted (client access ports). DHCP Offer/Ack messages from untrusted ports are dropped. It also builds a binding table used by DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection).

questions.question: What does Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) protect against?
options: Duplicate IP addresses on the same subnet
questions.explanation: DAI prevents ARP spoofing/poisoning by validating ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table (IP-to-MAC-to-port mappings). If an ARP reply's IP-to-MAC mapping doesn't match the binding table, the packet is dropped. This prevents attackers from redirecting traffic by sending false ARP replies.

questions.question: What is required before you can generate RSA keys for SSH on a Cisco IOS device?
options: An enable secret must be set
A hostname and domain name must be configured
VTY lines must be configured for local login
A crypto policy must be applied
questions.explanation: To use SSH on Cisco IOS, you must: 1) Set a hostname (not 'Router'), 2) Set a domain name (`ip domain-name`), 3) Generate RSA keys (`crypto key generate rsa`), 4) Configure VTY lines to use SSH (`transport input ssh`) with local or AAA authentication. The hostname + domain name are required because they form the RSA key name.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key difference between RADIUS and TACACS+?
options: RADIUS uses TCP; TACACS+ uses UDP
TACACS+ encrypts the entire payload; RADIUS encrypts only the password
RADIUS supports command authorization; TACACS+ does not
TACACS+ is an open standard; RADIUS is proprietary
questions.explanation: TACACS+ (TCP 49) encrypts the entire packet body, making it more secure. RADIUS (UDP 1812/1813) only encrypts the password field. TACACS+ separates Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting into independent functions. RADIUS combines Authentication and Authorization. TACACS+ is Cisco proprietary; RADIUS is an IETF standard.

questions.question: What authentication method does WPA3-Personal use instead of the Pre-Shared Key (PSK) handshake?
questions.explanation: WPA3-Personal replaces the PSK 4-way handshake with Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE), also known as Dragonfly. SAE provides forward secrecy and is resistant to offline dictionary attacks — even if an attacker captures the handshake, they cannot brute-force the password offline.

questions.question: What is a zero-day vulnerability?
options: A vulnerability that has been known for exactly one day
A vulnerability that is unknown to the vendor and has no available patch
A vulnerability that only affects old (zero-support) software
A vulnerability that requires zero user interaction to exploit
questions.explanation: A zero-day vulnerability is a software flaw that is unknown to the software vendor and/or the public, for which no patch or fix is available. Attackers who discover zero-days can exploit them with no defense available until the vendor releases a patch. The term refers to the 'zero days' the vendor has had to fix it.

questions.question: What is the key operational difference between an IDS and an IPS?
options: IDS uses signatures; IPS uses anomaly detection
IDS is inline and can block traffic; IPS only monitors and alerts
IDS passively monitors and alerts; IPS is inline and can block traffic
questions.explanation: IDS (Intrusion Detection System) operates out-of-band — it receives a copy of traffic, detects threats, and generates alerts but cannot block traffic. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is inline — all traffic passes through it, allowing it to detect AND actively block/drop malicious traffic in real time.

questions.question: What is VLAN hopping and how does double-tagging exploit it?
options: Moving a port between VLANs; double-tagging adds two 802.1Q headers to bypass trunk filtering
Creating VLANs from the CLI; double-tagging uses two VLAN IDs simultaneously
Trunking between VLANs; double-tagging uses STP to hop between VLANs
Accessing VLANs via SSH; double-tagging exploits SSH key weakness
questions.explanation: VLAN hopping allows traffic to reach unauthorized VLANs. Double-tagging: attacker sends a frame with two 802.1Q tags (outer = native VLAN, inner = target VLAN). The first switch strips the outer tag (native VLAN is untagged on trunks) and forwards the inner-tagged frame to the target VLAN. Mitigation: change the native VLAN to an unused VLAN.

questions.question: What happens to traffic that does not match any ACL entry?
options: It is dropped by the implicit deny any
It is sent to the next router for processing
questions.explanation: Every Cisco ACL has an implicit 'deny any' (deny all) at the end that is not shown in the configuration. Traffic that does not match any explicit permit or deny statement is dropped. This is a critical security principle — if you apply an ACL and don't have at least one permit statement, ALL traffic will be blocked.

questions.question: Which wireless security protocol provides the strongest encryption for enterprise networks?
questions.explanation: Security ranking strongest to weakest: WPA3 > WPA2 (AES/CCMP) > WPA (TKIP) > WEP. WEP uses RC4 with known vulnerabilities and should never be used. WPA uses TKIP (still RC4 based). WPA2 uses AES-based CCMP, which is currently strong. WPA3 adds SAE and 192-bit security suite for enhanced protection.

questions.question: What is a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security?
options: A military-restricted network zone with no civilian access
A network segment between internal and external firewalls, hosting public-facing services
A VLAN reserved for security monitoring tools
A NAT zone where private IPs are demilitarized
questions.explanation: A DMZ is a network segment that sits between two firewalls (or uses a multi-homed firewall). It hosts servers that need to be accessible from the Internet (web servers, mail servers, DNS) while keeping the internal corporate network isolated. Traffic from the Internet can reach the DMZ but not directly reach the internal network.

questions.question: What does enabling BPDU Guard on an access port do?
options: Prevents the port from ever forwarding BPDUs to other switches
Places the port in err-disable state if a BPDU is received on it
Enables the port to become a root port faster
Applies spanning tree PortFast on the port
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard protects PortFast-enabled access ports. If a BPDU is received on a BPDU Guard port (meaning someone connected a switch or rogue device), the port is immediately placed in err-disable state. This prevents someone from connecting an unauthorized switch that could disrupt the STP topology. Configured with `spanning-tree bpduguard enable` or globally.

questions.question: What is a MAC flooding attack and how does it work?
options: Flooding the network with broadcast MAC frames to exhaust bandwidth
Sending many frames with different source MACs to fill the CAM table, causing the switch to act as a hub
Sending ARP replies with a spoofed MAC to redirect traffic
Using CDP to flood network topology information
questions.explanation: MAC flooding fills the switch's CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table with fake MAC addresses. When the table is full, the switch cannot learn new MACs and must flood all frames to all ports (fail-open), allowing the attacker to capture traffic. Mitigation: port security (`switchport port-security maximum 1`) limits MACs per port.

questions.question: What is reconnaissance in the context of network security?
options: An attack that floods a network with traffic
The initial information-gathering phase where attackers discover targets and vulnerabilities
Unauthorized physical access to network infrastructure
questions.explanation: Reconnaissance (recon) is the first phase of most attacks — gathering information about the target. Passive recon: searching public records (WHOIS, DNS, LinkedIn) without touching the target. Active recon: port scanning (nmap), vulnerability scanning, ping sweeps. This phase identifies attack vectors before exploitation.

questions.question: What is the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption?
options: Symmetric is stronger; asymmetric is weaker
Symmetric uses the same key for encryption/decryption; asymmetric uses a public/private key pair
Symmetric is used for digital signatures; asymmetric is for bulk data encryption
They are the same algorithm with different key lengths
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption (fast, used for bulk data) — AES, 3DES. Asymmetric uses a key pair: encrypt with public key, decrypt with private key (or sign with private, verify with public) — RSA, ECC (slower, used for key exchange and signatures). TLS uses asymmetric for key exchange, then symmetric for data.

questions.question: Why is Telnet considered insecure and what should replace it?
options: Telnet is slow; SSH should replace it for performance
Telnet transmits all data including passwords in plaintext; SSH encrypts all traffic
Telnet uses UDP which is unreliable; SSH uses TCP
Telnet is deprecated; RDP should replace it
questions.explanation: Telnet sends all data, including usernames and passwords, in cleartext that can be captured by a network sniffer. SSH (Secure Shell) uses asymmetric key exchange + symmetric encryption to secure all traffic. Minimum SSH version 2 should be used. Configure: `transport input ssh` on VTY lines, `ip ssh version 2`. Block Telnet with `transport input none`.

questions.question: What is defense in depth?
options: Using very deep packet inspection on a single firewall
Layering multiple security controls so that if one fails, others still protect the network
Deploying firewalls only at the network perimeter
Using deep learning AI for intrusion detection
questions.explanation: Defense in depth is a security strategy that uses multiple overlapping layers of security controls. If one layer fails (e.g., a perimeter firewall is breached), the attacker still faces additional barriers (IPS, 802.1X, host-based firewalls, network segmentation, encryption). Also called 'layered security.' Contrasts with perimeter-only security.

questions.question: What is the default violation mode for port security on Cisco switches?
questions.explanation: Default port security violation mode is Shutdown — the port is placed in err-disable state if a violation occurs. Violation modes: Shutdown = err-disable port + syslog + SNMP trap. Restrict = drop offending frames + increment counter + syslog/SNMP. Protect = silently drop offending frames, no counter/log. Recover from err-disable: `shutdown` then `no shutdown` or configure `errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation`.

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization in network security?
options: Authentication determines what you can do; authorization verifies who you are
Authentication verifies identity (who you are); authorization determines permissions (what you can do)
They are the same process performed at different OSI layers
Authentication uses RADIUS; authorization always uses TACACS+
questions.explanation: Authentication = verifying identity ('prove who you are') — username/password, certificates, biometrics. Authorization = determining access rights ('what are you allowed to do') — VLAN assignment, command privilege levels, ACL access. Accounting = tracking what authenticated users did. All three make up AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting).

questions.question: How do you recover a port from err-disable state caused by a port security violation?
options: The port recovers automatically after 300 seconds
Use `no shutdown` followed by `shutdown` on the port, or configure `errdisable recovery`
Remove and re-add the port security configuration
questions.explanation: To recover from err-disable: manually use `shutdown` then `no shutdown` on the interface. Or configure automatic recovery: `errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation` and `errdisable recovery interval <seconds>`. The violation must be resolved first (remove unauthorized device). Check port status with `show port-security interface <id>` and `show interfaces <id> status`.

questions.question: What does the `service password-encryption` command do on a Cisco router?
options: Encrypts enable password, VTY, and console passwords with a weak reversible cipher (type 7)
Enables SSH encryption on VTY lines
questions.explanation: `service password-encryption` applies Cisco's type 7 (Vigenere cipher) encryption to plain-text passwords in the config (enable password, line passwords). This is weak — easily decoded with online tools. `enable secret` uses irreversible MD5 (type 5) and is always encrypted regardless. For maximum security: use `enable secret` and `service password-encryption` together.

questions.question: What is a Denial of Service (DoS) attack?
options: Stealing encrypted data from a network
Overwhelming a target with traffic or requests to make it unavailable
Gaining unauthorized access to a network device
questions.explanation: A DoS attack overwhelms a target (server, router, firewall) with excessive traffic or malformed packets, exhausting resources (CPU, bandwidth, memory) and making the service unavailable to legitimate users. DDoS (Distributed DoS) uses many compromised systems (botnet) to amplify the attack. Mitigation: rate limiting, ACLs, black hole routing, DDoS scrubbing services.

questions.question: Which of the following are recommended security hardening practices for Cisco routers? (Select the best answer)
options: Enable Telnet, disable SSH, use simple passwords
Disable unused services (CDP on WAN links), use SSH v2, configure AAA, set minimum password length
Leave all default settings and only change passwords
Enable all routing protocols to ensure redundancy
questions.explanation: Router hardening best practices: Use SSH v2 (not Telnet). Configure `service password-encryption` + `enable secret`. Disable unused services (`no cdp enable` on WAN interfaces, `no ip proxy-arp`). Configure AAA with RADIUS/TACACS+. Set minimum password requirements. Configure login banners. Use VTY ACLs. Enable `login block-for` (brute force protection). Audit with `show run`.

questions.question: What protocols make up IPsec VPN?
options: AH (Authentication Header) and ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)
questions.explanation: IPsec consists of: AH (Authentication Header) — provides authentication and integrity (no encryption), ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) — provides encryption + authentication + integrity. IKE (Internet Key Exchange) negotiates security associations. IPsec modes: Transport (encrypts payload only) and Tunnel (encrypts entire original packet, adds new IP header). Most VPNs use ESP in Tunnel mode.

questions.question: What is the purpose of NAC (Network Access Control)?
options: Translating private to public IP addresses at the network edge
Verifying endpoint compliance (OS patches, antivirus) before granting network access
Controlling ACL entries on network devices
questions.explanation: NAC (Network Access Control) verifies that endpoints meet security requirements before granting network access — checking OS patch levels, antivirus updates, firewall status. Non-compliant devices are quarantined or given limited access. 802.1X is the port-based NAC standard. Cisco ISE (Identity Services Engine) is a popular NAC solution. Prevents compromised endpoints from infecting the network.

questions.question: What is the role of a Certificate Authority (CA) in PKI?
options: The CA encrypts all network traffic
The CA is a trusted third party that issues and signs digital certificates, verifying public key ownership
The CA manages user passwords and authentication tokens
The CA provides the private key to each user
questions.explanation: PKI (Public Key Infrastructure): A CA is a trusted entity that issues digital certificates. Certificates bind a public key to an identity (domain name, organization). Process: entity generates key pair → submits CSR to CA → CA verifies identity and issues signed certificate. Clients trust certificates signed by trusted CAs. Used in TLS/HTTPS, SSH, 802.1X, VPN. Root CAs sign intermediate CAs.

questions.question: What command sequence configures a Cisco router to authenticate management access via RADIUS server 10.1.1.100 with key 'cisco123'?
options:
radius server 10.1.1.100 key cisco123
aaa authentication login default radius local

aaa new-model
radius-server host 10.1.1.100 key cisco123
aaa authentication login default group radius local
questions.explanation: AAA/RADIUS configuration: 1) `aaa new-model` (enables AAA). 2) `radius-server host <ip> key <key>` (or newer: `radius server <name>`, then `address ipv4 <ip>`, `key <key>`). 3) `aaa authentication login default group radius local` (use RADIUS first, fall back to local database). 4) `aaa authorization exec default group radius local` (optional, for authorization).

questions.question: Which command verifies that SSH is configured and shows the SSH version?
questions.explanation: `show ip ssh` displays: SSH version, authentication timeout, retries, and whether SSH is enabled. `show ssh` shows active SSH sessions. To configure SSH: hostname, ip domain-name, crypto key generate rsa (modulus ≥ 1024), username <name> privilege 15 secret <pw>, line vty 0 15, transport input ssh, login local.

questions.question: What is the minimum recommended wireless security for an enterprise network?
questions.explanation: WPA2 with AES/CCMP is the minimum acceptable security standard for enterprise wireless. WEP is completely broken and should never be used. WPA-TKIP has known weaknesses. WPA2-Personal uses PSK (vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks if weak passphrase). WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X/EAP with RADIUS for individual user authentication. WPA3 improves on WPA2 but many devices still only support WPA2.

questions.question: What commands are needed to configure DHCP snooping on a switch for VLAN 10?
options:
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10
questions.explanation: DHCP snooping configuration: 1) `ip dhcp snooping` (enable globally), 2) `ip dhcp snooping vlan 10` (enable for specific VLAN). Then on trusted interfaces (uplink to DHCP server): `ip dhcp snooping trust`. Untrusted ports (user-facing) have rate limiting option: `ip dhcp snooping limit rate <pps>`. Verify: `show ip dhcp snooping` and `show ip dhcp snooping binding`.

questions.question: What is the purpose of IKE (Internet Key Exchange) in IPsec?
options: IKE encrypts the data payload in an IPsec tunnel
IKE negotiates Security Associations (SAs) and exchanges encryption keys for IPsec
IKE provides the authentication certificate for IPsec connections
IKE monitors the IPsec tunnel health and re-establishes it if it fails
questions.explanation: IKE (Internet Key Exchange) — uses UDP port 500/4500. Phase 1: establishes a secure, authenticated channel between peers (using pre-shared key or RSA certificates). Phase 2: uses the Phase 1 channel to negotiate the IPsec SA (encryption algorithm, authentication, keys, lifetime). IKEv2 is faster and more resilient than IKEv1. Without IKE, keys would have to be manually configured.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a network vulnerability scanner?
options: To actively block network attacks in real time
To proactively identify security weaknesses and misconfigurations before attackers do
To test application performance under load
questions.explanation: A vulnerability scanner (e.g., Nessus, OpenVAS) proactively discovers security weaknesses: open ports, unpatched software, weak passwords, misconfigurations, default credentials. Different from IPS (which blocks attacks in real time). Results used to prioritize remediation. Penetration testing takes it further: actively exploits vulnerabilities to assess impact. Part of a defense-in-depth security program.

questions.question: What is 'vishing'?
options: A visual phishing attack using fake websites
Social engineering via phone calls to trick victims into revealing information
Sending SMS messages with malicious links
questions.explanation: Social engineering attack types: Phishing = deceptive email. Spear phishing = targeted phishing. Whaling = targeting executives. Vishing = voice phishing (phone calls). Smishing = SMS phishing. Pretexting = fabricating a scenario to gain trust. Baiting = leaving infected USB drives. Tailgating/piggybacking = physical access by following authorized person. All exploit human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is CoPP (Control Plane Policing)?
options: A mechanism to encrypt control plane traffic between routers
Rate-limiting traffic destined for the router's CPU to protect it from DoS attacks
A policy to control which routing protocols can run on an interface
An ACL applied to management interfaces to restrict SSH access
questions.explanation: CoPP (Control Plane Policing) protects the router's CPU from being overwhelmed by excessive control plane traffic — whether from routing protocol floods, ICMP storms, or DoS attacks. Uses MQC (Modular QoS CLI) to classify and rate-limit traffic going to the control plane. Critical for protecting production routers. Traffic classes: routing protocols, ICMP, SNMP, SSH, Telnet. Drop or police excess.

questions.question: What is a deauthentication attack in wireless networks?
options: Flooding a wireless network with association requests
Sending forged deauthentication frames to disconnect clients from an AP
Cracking WPA2 handshakes with offline dictionary attacks
Setting up a rogue AP to capture credentials
questions.explanation: A deauthentication (deauth) attack exploits the fact that 802.11 management frames (including deauthentication) are not authenticated in WPA2. An attacker sends forged deauth frames, forcing clients to disconnect from their AP. This causes service disruption (DoS) and can also be used to capture WPA2 4-way handshakes (for offline cracking). 802.11w (MFP - Management Frame Protection) mitigates this.

questions.question: What is endpoint posture assessment in NAC?
options: Checking the physical position of network devices for cable management
Evaluating whether an endpoint meets security requirements (OS patches, AV status) before granting access
Assessing the network position (VLAN) of an endpoint
Evaluating the risk score of network traffic flows
questions.explanation: Endpoint posture assessment checks if a connecting device meets security requirements: OS is up-to-date, antivirus is running and current, host-based firewall is enabled, specific software is or isn't installed. Non-compliant devices are quarantined in a remediation VLAN. Cisco ISE performs posture assessment using the NAC agent or Temporal Agent. Part of Zero Trust security model.

questions.question: Which encryption algorithm is considered the current standard for wireless and VPN encryption?
questions.explanation: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) is the current gold standard: 128, 192, or 256-bit keys. Used in WPA2/WPA3, IPsec VPN, TLS 1.2/1.3. DES (56-bit) is broken — crackable in hours. 3DES applies DES three times (168-bit effective, ~112-bit security) — deprecated in modern standards. RC4 is broken (used in WEP and old TLS). CCMP (WPA2) = AES in Counter Mode with CBC-MAC.

questions.question: What does TLS (Transport Layer Security) provide?
options: Authentication only — verifies server identity with certificates
Encryption, authentication, and data integrity for TCP-based communication
Physical layer encryption for WAN links
questions.explanation: TLS (successor to SSL) provides three security services over TCP: Authentication (server certificate proves identity via PKI), Confidentiality (symmetric encryption after key exchange — AES, ChaCha20), Integrity (HMAC prevents tampering). TLS 1.3 (current standard) improved over 1.2 with fewer round trips, stronger cipher suites, and eliminated weak options. Used for HTTPS, SMTP/TLS, FTPS, SSH alternative.

questions.question: When is WPA2-Enterprise preferred over WPA2-Personal for wireless security?
options: When fewer than 10 users connect to the wireless network
In enterprise environments where individual user authentication is needed (802.1X/RADIUS) rather than a shared PSK
When the network uses 5 GHz frequency only
When the AP cannot support AES encryption
questions.explanation: WPA2-Personal (PSK): all users share the same pre-shared key — simpler but if compromised, all users must change password and the key is vulnerable to offline attacks. WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X): each user authenticates with unique credentials (username/password or certificates) via RADIUS. If one account is compromised, only that account is revoked. Preferred for corporate environments. Also provides per-user session keys.

questions.question: What is the trust boundary in a QoS design?
options: The point where QoS markings from endpoints are accepted or re-marked
questions.explanation: The trust boundary is where the network decides to trust or override QoS markings set by end devices. Typically placed at IP phones or access switches.

questions.question: Which IPsec protocol provides data confidentiality (encryption) for VPN traffic?
questions.explanation: ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) provides encryption, authentication, and integrity. AH provides authentication and integrity but NOT encryption.

questions.question: What port and protocol does IKE (used for IPsec VPN negotiation) use?
questions.explanation: IKE uses UDP 500 for initial negotiation. IKE NAT-T (NAT Traversal) uses UDP 4500 when NAT is detected in the path.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of a site-to-site VPN over a remote-access VPN?
options: Site-to-site VPNs require client software on each user PC
Site-to-site VPNs connect entire networks without per-user software
Site-to-site VPNs use SSL instead of IPsec
questions.explanation: Site-to-site VPNs connect two networks (e.g., branch to HQ) using router/firewall endpoints — no per-user client software needed.

questions.question: What does AAA stand for in network security?
questions.explanation: AAA = Authentication (verify identity), Authorization (what resources are allowed), Accounting (track what was done). Framework used by RADIUS and TACACS+.

questions.question: Which AAA protocol encrypts the entire packet payload and is Cisco proprietary?
questions.explanation: TACACS+ (TCP 49) encrypts the entire body of the AAA packet. RADIUS (UDP 1812/1813) only encrypts the password field.

questions.question: What transport protocol and port does RADIUS use for authentication?
questions.explanation: Modern RADIUS uses UDP 1812 for authentication and UDP 1813 for accounting. Older implementations may use UDP 1645/1646.

questions.question: What is the role of the authenticator in 802.1X?
options: The network device (switch/AP) that enforces access control
questions.explanation: In 802.1X: Supplicant=end device, Authenticator=switch or AP that controls port access, Authentication Server=RADIUS server that verifies identity.

questions.question: What happens to a switch port when port security detects a violation in 'shutdown' mode?
options: The offending frame is dropped silently
The port is placed in err-disabled state and a syslog message is generated
The MAC address is added to the allowed list
The port is placed in restrict mode
questions.explanation: Shutdown mode (default) err-disables the port and generates a syslog alert when a violation occurs. The port must be manually re-enabled with `shutdown` then `no shutdown`.

questions.question: What does 'sticky' MAC address learning do in port security?
options: Manually configures allowed MACs in config
Dynamically learns MACs and saves them to running-config
Blocks all MACs except the first one learned
Resets learned MACs after a timer
questions.explanation: Sticky learning dynamically learns the first MAC(s) seen on the port and adds them to the running-config as secure MACs. They persist after `copy running-config startup-config`.

questions.question: What does Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) use to validate ARP packets?
questions.explanation: DAI checks ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table (which maps IP-to-MAC-to-port). ARP replies that don't match are dropped.

questions.question: Which attack technique allows a host on VLAN 10 to send traffic to VLAN 20 by manipulating 802.1Q tags?
questions.explanation: Double-tagging VLAN hopping exploits the native VLAN by sending frames with two 802.1Q tags. The outer tag (native VLAN) is stripped by the first switch, and the inner tag delivers the frame to the target VLAN.

questions.question: Which mitigation prevents a rogue switch from becoming a DTP trunk partner?
questions.explanation: `switchport nonegotiate` disables DTP on the port, preventing any device from negotiating a trunk — mitigating switch spoofing VLAN hopping.

questions.question: What authentication improvement does WPA3 introduce over WPA2?
options: Replaces PSK with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals)
Removes the need for a passphrase
questions.explanation: WPA3 uses SAE (Dragonfly handshake) instead of PSK, providing forward secrecy and resistance to offline dictionary attacks.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Control Plane Policing (CoPP)?
options: Rate-limits traffic destined to the router CPU to prevent DoS
Filters data plane traffic with ACLs
questions.explanation: CoPP protects the router CPU (control plane) from being overwhelmed by applying rate-limiting policies to traffic directed at the router itself (routing protocol packets, management traffic, etc.).

questions.question: What is the current symmetric encryption standard recommended for VPN and wireless security?
questions.explanation: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) replaced DES/3DES as the standard. It supports 128, 192, or 256-bit keys and is used in WPA2/WPA3, IPsec, and TLS.

questions.question: Which PPP sub-protocol negotiates Layer 3 protocol parameters (e.g., IP address assignment)?
questions.explanation: NCP (Network Control Protocol) negotiates Layer 3 parameters for each routed protocol. IPCP is the NCP for IP. LCP establishes, configures, and tests the data link connection.

questions.question: Why is CHAP more secure than PAP for PPP authentication?
options: CHAP encrypts the username and password; PAP does not
CHAP uses a three-way challenge-response; PAP sends credentials in cleartext
CHAP uses AES; PAP uses MD5
CHAP is bidirectional; PAP is not
questions.explanation: CHAP uses an MD5 challenge-response (three-way handshake) and never sends the password across the link. PAP sends username/password in plaintext during establishment.

questions.question: What device in an MPLS network attaches and removes labels to/from packets at the edge?
questions.explanation: LER (Label Edge Router) = PE router. It imposes labels on ingress and removes them on egress. P routers (core) only switch based on labels without examining the IP header.

questions.question: Which WAN technology uses the existing telephone copper infrastructure and is asymmetric (faster download than upload)?
questions.explanation: ADSL (Asymmetric DSL) uses copper phone lines. Download speed is faster than upload speed. Distance from the DSLAM limits speed.

questions.question: What limitation does GRE have compared to IPsec tunnels?
options: GRE does not provide any encryption or authentication by default
GRE requires a dedicated hardware module
questions.explanation: GRE tunnels encapsulate any Layer 3 protocol but provide no encryption or authentication. IPsec is typically added on top of GRE to provide security (GRE over IPsec).

questions.question: Which type of attack involves sending unsolicited traffic to exhaust a network resource?
questions.explanation: A DoS (Denial of Service) attack floods a target with traffic or requests to exhaust resources and make services unavailable to legitimate users.

questions.question: What distinguishes a DDoS attack from a DoS attack?
options: DDoS is encrypted; DoS is not
DDoS uses multiple source systems simultaneously; DoS uses a single source
DDoS targets DNS only; DoS targets all protocols
DDoS is a Layer 7 attack; DoS is Layer 3
questions.explanation: A DDoS (Distributed DoS) uses a botnet of thousands of compromised hosts to overwhelm the target, making mitigation much harder than a single-source DoS.

questions.question: Which type of malware disguises itself as legitimate software while enabling unauthorized access?
questions.explanation: A Trojan horse appears to be legitimate software but contains hidden malicious functionality. Unlike viruses, Trojans do not self-replicate. Unlike worms, they require user execution.

questions.question: Which IOS command applies an ACL to a VTY (Telnet/SSH) line to restrict management access?
questions.explanation: `access-class <ACL> in` applied under `line vty 0 4` restricts which source IPs can connect via Telnet or SSH. `ip access-group` is used on physical interfaces.

questions.question: What is the advantage of SSH over Telnet for remote router management?
options: SSH encrypts the entire session; Telnet sends data in cleartext
questions.explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) provides encrypted authentication and session data. Telnet transmits everything — including passwords — in plaintext, making it vulnerable to packet capture.

questions.question: Which IOS command generates the RSA key pair required for SSH on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `crypto key generate rsa modulus <bits>` generates the RSA key pair. SSH also requires: `ip domain-name`, a local user, `ip ssh version 2`, and `line vty login local`.

questions.question: What is defense-in-depth?
options: Layering multiple security controls so that failure of one doesn't compromise the entire system
Encrypting all traffic at the perimeter
questions.explanation: Defense-in-depth (layered security) uses multiple overlapping security controls — firewalls, IPS, AAA, encryption, physical security — so attackers must defeat multiple independent layers.

questions.question: What is Metro Ethernet (MetroE) and what advantage does it offer over traditional WAN?
options: Ethernet-based WAN service using familiar interface standards and typically higher bandwidth at lower cost
A DSL variant for metropolitan areas
questions.explanation: Metro Ethernet extends Ethernet-based connectivity across a metropolitan area. It uses familiar Ethernet interfaces (no serial/WAN card needed) and typically offers higher bandwidth at lower cost than traditional Frame Relay/ATM.

questions.question: What is the primary business driver for SD-WAN adoption?
options: Using multiple WAN links (MPLS, internet, LTE) intelligently based on application policy, reducing cost
questions.explanation: SD-WAN uses software policy to intelligently route traffic across multiple WAN transports (dedicated MPLS, public internet, 4G/LTE), optimizing cost and performance. Applications get the best path automatically.

questions.question: What does 'line protocol is down' on a serial interface typically indicate?
options: The interface is administratively shut down
Layer 2 (keepalives/encapsulation) issue — often a clock rate mismatch or encapsulation mismatch
questions.explanation: 'Line protocol down' = Layer 2 failure. On serial links: missing clock rate on DCE end, PPP/HDLC encapsulation mismatch, or missing keepalives. 'Interface down' = physical layer issue.

questions.question: In MPLS Layer 3 VPN, what is the role of the PE (Provider Edge) router?
options: Forwards labeled packets between P routers
Connects directly to the customer CE router, adds/removes MPLS labels, maintains per-VPN routing tables (VRFs)
Acts as the DNS server for the MPLS network
questions.explanation: PE routers interface with customer CE routers. They maintain separate VRF (Virtual Routing and Forwarding) tables per customer and impose/remove MPLS labels. P routers only forward labeled packets.

questions.question: What does 'asymmetric' mean in ADSL broadband?
options: Upload and download speeds are equal
Download speed is faster than upload speed
The connection uses two separate lines
questions.explanation: In ADSL, download speed exceeds upload speed. This matches typical home/office use (more download than upload). SDSL provides symmetric speeds at higher cost.

questions.question: Which WAN technology uses a shared coaxial cable network with a cable modem and DOCSIS standard?
questions.explanation: Cable broadband uses the CATV coaxial cable infrastructure and DOCSIS (Data Over Cable Service Interface Specification). Bandwidth is shared among subscribers in a neighborhood.

questions.question: What type of VPN is typically used to securely connect an individual remote worker to a corporate network?
questions.explanation: Remote-access VPN (e.g., Cisco AnyConnect with IPsec/SSL) allows individual users to connect securely to the corporate network from home or travel, requiring client software.

questions.question: Which PPP feature allows two routers to authenticate each other bidirectionally?
questions.explanation: CHAP supports mutual (bidirectional) authentication where each peer challenges the other. PAP only authenticates the caller (one-way). CHAP uses MD5 challenge-response, never sending the password.

questions.question: On a back-to-back serial connection in a lab, which end must configure the clock rate?
questions.explanation: The DCE end (typically the router connected to a DCE cable) must configure `clock rate` on the serial interface. The DTE end receives the clock. In production, the service provider's CSU/DSU provides the clock.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of MPLS over traditional IP routing for enterprise WAN?
options: MPLS is cheaper than internet-based VPNs
MPLS provides guaranteed SLAs, QoS, and can be configured as an any-to-any Layer 2 or Layer 3 VPN
MPLS eliminates the need for routers at customer sites
questions.explanation: MPLS provides carrier-grade SLAs with guaranteed latency, jitter, and packet loss — critical for voice/video. It supports L2VPN and L3VPN, enabling any-to-any connectivity between sites.

questions.question: Which WAN solution provides the highest bandwidth at the lowest latency for a data center interconnect within the same city?
questions.explanation: Dark fiber (dedicated unlit fiber leased from a carrier) or Metro Ethernet provides extremely high bandwidth and low latency for local/metropolitan data center interconnects.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Network Access Control (NAC) with posture assessment?
options: Assigns VLANs based on switch port number
Verifies that endpoint devices meet security policy requirements (AV, patches) before granting network access
Provides DHCP to wireless clients only
questions.explanation: NAC checks device posture (antivirus status, OS patches, firewall state) before admission. Non-compliant devices are quarantined or denied — preventing compromised devices from joining the network.

questions.question: What is a deauthentication attack in wireless networking?
options: Sending spoofed deauth frames to disconnect clients from a legitimate AP, often preceding an evil twin attack
Intercepting DHCP traffic to assign rogue IPs
Flooding the wireless channel with data frames
questions.explanation: A deauth attack sends forged 802.11 deauthentication frames to kick clients off their AP. Attackers use it to force clients to reconnect to an evil twin AP they control.

questions.question: What is a stateful firewall and how does it differ from a stateless (packet filter) firewall?
options: A stateful firewall processes packets faster
A stateful firewall tracks connection state and only allows return traffic for established sessions; a packet filter evaluates each packet independently
A stateless firewall is more secure
A stateful firewall operates at Layer 2 only
questions.explanation: Stateful firewalls maintain a connection state table. Return traffic matching an established session is automatically permitted without an explicit rule. Stateless ACLs evaluate each packet individually regardless of session context.

questions.question: Which physical security control is most effective at preventing unauthorized access to a data center?
options: Mantrap / badge-access door with video surveillance
questions.explanation: Physical security controls like mantraps, biometric/badge access, and video surveillance prevent unauthorized physical access to networking equipment — the most fundamental layer of defense-in-depth.

questions.question: What security weakness does WPA2-Personal (PSK) have that WPA2-Enterprise does not?
options: WPA2-Personal uses a shared passphrase for all users — if one user is compromised, all must change the passphrase
questions.explanation: WPA2-Personal uses a single PSK shared by all users. WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X with individual user credentials (RADIUS), so a single compromised user doesn't affect others and their access can be revoked individually.

questions.question: What is the 'protect' violation mode in port security?
options: Drops violating frames and sends an SNMP trap
Drops violating frames silently — no log or trap
Drops violating frames and increments the violation counter only
questions.explanation: Protect mode silently drops frames from unauthorized MACs without logging or sending traps. Restrict mode drops frames AND logs/increments counter. Shutdown mode err-disables the port.

questions.question: Which management protocols use encryption to protect data in transit?
questions.explanation: SSH (replaces Telnet) and HTTPS (replaces HTTP) encrypt management traffic. Telnet, HTTP, SNMPv1/v2c, and TFTP transmit data in cleartext.

questions.question: Which security best practice should be applied to management interfaces (VTY lines)?
options: Allow all IP addresses for management flexibility
Use ACLs to restrict access to management IPs only and use SSH instead of Telnet
questions.explanation: VTY lines should be restricted with `access-class` ACLs to only permit known management stations, combined with SSH (not Telnet) and strong authentication — following least-privilege principles.

questions.question: What is co-channel interference in wireless networking?
options: Interference from non-Wi-Fi devices like microwaves
Interference between APs using the same channel, reducing throughput
A security attack on the wireless channel
questions.explanation: Co-channel interference occurs when multiple APs in the same area use the same channel. All APs on the same channel must contend for medium access, reducing overall throughput. Use non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11).

questions.question: What is a botnet?
options: A network of compromised computers (bots) controlled by an attacker for DDoS, spam, or credential theft
questions.explanation: A botnet is a collection of internet-connected devices infected with malware and controlled remotely by a threat actor (bot herder). Used to launch DDoS attacks, send spam, or mine cryptocurrency.

questions.question: What is ransomware?
options: Malware that encrypts victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts files on infected systems and demands a ransom (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Often spreads via phishing emails or exploit kits.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using out-of-band (OOB) management?
options: Management traffic uses a separate dedicated network, so it remains accessible even during a production network outage
Provides higher bandwidth for device configuration
questions.explanation: OOB management uses a dedicated management network (separate from production) — often via console server or dedicated management interfaces. Devices can be accessed even when production links fail.

questions.question: What is a T1 line and what is its bandwidth?
options: A fiber optic WAN line with 100 Mbps
A digital WAN circuit composed of 24 DS0 channels with 1.544 Mbps aggregate bandwidth
A satellite connection with 1.5 Mbps download
questions.explanation: A T1 (DS1) is a digital WAN circuit with 24 DS0 (64 Kbps) channels = 1.544 Mbps total. E1 (European equivalent) = 32 channels = 2.048 Mbps. T3 = 28 T1s = 44.736 Mbps.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a CSU/DSU in WAN connectivity?
options: Converts digital data from the router's serial interface to the digital signal format used on the WAN circuit
Acts as a wireless access point for WAN connectivity
questions.explanation: CSU/DSU (Channel Service Unit/Data Service Unit) connects a router's serial interface to the WAN provider circuit (T1/E1). It converts between the router's internal signaling and the carrier's line format, and provides clocking.

questions.question: What happens to a switch port in 802.1X before the supplicant authenticates?
options: The port immediately goes to forwarding
The port is placed in the unauthorized state and only EAP traffic is allowed
The port sends DHCP to the client
questions.explanation: Before 802.1X authentication, the switch port is in unauthorized state — only EAP (authentication) traffic passes. After successful RADIUS authentication, the port transitions to authorized state for normal traffic.

questions.question: What replaced SNMPv1/v2c for secure network management?
questions.explanation: SNMPv3 with authPriv provides authentication (SHA/MD5) and encryption (AES/DES) — addressing SNMPv1/v2c's cleartext community strings. SNMPv3 is required in any security-conscious environment.

questions.question: What does 'least privilege' mean as a security principle?
options: Users should have no access by default
Users and systems should be granted only the minimum access required to perform their job function
All users share the same privilege level
questions.explanation: Least privilege limits access rights to the minimum necessary — reducing attack surface. If an account is compromised, the attacker has limited capabilities. Applied to users, processes, and network segments.

questions.question: What is the difference between tunnel mode and transport mode in IPsec?
options: Tunnel mode is faster; transport mode is more secure
Tunnel mode encapsulates the entire original IP packet with a new IP header; transport mode only encrypts the payload, keeping the original IP header
Transport mode is for site-to-site; tunnel mode is for remote access
questions.explanation: IPsec Tunnel mode adds a new outer IP header — used for VPNs between gateways (the inner IP is hidden). Transport mode encrypts only the payload, using the original IP header — used for host-to-host encryption.

questions.question: Which WAN technology provides low latency but very high deployment cost for last-mile connectivity?
questions.explanation: FTTP/FTTH (Fiber to the Premises/Home) offers the lowest latency and highest bandwidth of any last-mile technology but requires significant civil engineering (trenching, cable installation), making deployment expensive.

questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of satellite internet for enterprise WAN use?
options: Very high latency (~600ms round trip) due to signal travel distance to geostationary orbit
questions.explanation: Geostationary satellites orbit at ~35,786 km, causing ~600ms round-trip latency. This makes real-time applications (VoIP, video conferencing) unusable and impacts TCP performance. Used only when no other option exists.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies port security configuration and violation counts on an interface?
questions.explanation: `show port-security interface <int>` displays: max MACs, current MACs, violation mode, violation count, and security status. `show port-security` shows a summary for all ports.
